findings.title: Score deduplication in rounds property diverges from events property — rounds is correct, events is wrong
findings.description: The engine's `round.scores` list contains duplicate player entries (one entry per trick won, not one per player). The `rounds` property correctly sums them: `score_totals[id] = score_totals.get(id, 0) + entry.value`. The pre-existing `events` property uses a dict comprehension `{s.identifier: s.value for s in ...}` which silently overwrites duplicates, producing wrong per-round scores. Verified empirically: Round 0 raw scores are `[('p4',5),('p4',10),('p1',5),('p2',5),('p2',5)]`; sum gives `{'p4':15,'p1':5,'p2':10}` (matches `g.scores` cumulative); overwrite gives `{'p4':10,'p1':5,'p2':5}` (wrong). The `rounds` property is correct. The `events` property has a pre-existing bug that this diff does not fix or worsen, but the divergence means `RoundEnd` events in the events stream carry incorrect scores while the spike endpoint carries correct ones — a silent inconsistency between the two endpoints.
findings.impact: The spike endpoint's per-round scores are correct and sum to the cumulative game scores. The existing `/events` endpoint's `ROUND_END` scores are systematically wrong (undercount). No crash; silent data corruption in the events endpoint only.
findings.recommendation: Fix the pre-existing `__events_for_action` bug: replace `{s.identifier: s.value for s in replay_engine.rounds[-2].scores}` with the same summing pattern used in `rounds`. This is out of scope for this diff but should be tracked.

findings.title: engine_trick.winning_play raises HundredAndTenError when plays=[] — guard is correct but fragile
findings.description: The engine's `trick.winning_play` property raises `HundredAndTenError` (not returns `None`) when `trick.plays` is empty. The code guards with `if engine_trick.plays:` at line 392, which correctly prevents reaching the `winning_play` access for empty tricks. However, inside that block the code then does `Play.from_engine(engine_trick.winning_play) if engine_trick.winning_play else None` — the `else None` branch is unreachable because `winning_play` raises rather than returning a falsy value when plays is empty, and the outer `if engine_trick.plays` already ensures plays is non-empty. The guard works correctly today, but the inner `if engine_trick.winning_play` creates a false impression that `winning_play` can be `None`/falsy for non-empty tricks. If the outer guard is ever removed or refactored, the inner guard will not catch the exception. Verified: `bool(t.winning_play)` raises `HundredAndTenError` when `plays=[]`.
findings.impact: No current crash — the outer guard is correct. Risk is future refactoring removes the outer guard while trusting the inner guard to protect, causing a `HundredAndTenError` in production.
findings.recommendation: In the active round section, replace `Play.from_engine(engine_trick.winning_play) if engine_trick.winning_play else None` with `Play.from_engine(engine_trick.winning_play)` (no guard needed since `plays` is non-empty). This makes the intent explicit. Same applies to the completed round section at line 361.

findings.title: Spectator (client_player_id not in game) silently degrades to all-counts view with no error
findings.description: When `client_player_id` is not in `m_game.ordered_players`, `m_game.ordered_players.find(client_player_id)` returns `None`. The code then evaluates `isinstance(None, internal.Human)` → `False`, so `queued = []`. The hands dict comprehension `pid == client_player_id` is never True, so all hands are returned as integer counts. No crash occurs. However, the route at `GET /players/{player_id}/games/{game_id}/spike` does not verify that `player_id` is actually a participant in the game — it only verifies the Firebase token matches `player_id`. A valid authenticated user who is not in the game receives a degraded but structurally valid response (all hands as counts, no queued actions) rather than a 403/404. This is consistent with the behavior of the existing `game_info` endpoint (which also does not enforce game membership), but it is worth noting as an intentional or accidental design choice.
findings.impact: No crash. Spectators receive a valid but privacy-degraded response. If the intent is to restrict the spike endpoint to participants only, this is a missing authorization check.
findings.recommendation: If participant-only access is intended, add a membership check (e.g., `if not m_game.ordered_players.find(client_player_id): raise AuthorizationError(...)`) consistent with how other routes handle this. If spectator access is acceptable, document the behavior.

findings.title: SelectTrump bidder fallback (bidder is None → set bidder) is dead code under current engine rules
findings.description: The code `if current_round.bidder is None: current_round.bidder = action.player_id` inside the `SelectTrump` match arm is intended to handle a case where `SelectTrump` is issued without a prior winning `Bid`. In Hundred and Ten, `SelectTrump` is only reachable after a winning bid (the engine enforces this). Empirical verification across 21 rounds of a completed game shows `SelectTrump` is always preceded by a `Bid` with `amount.value > 0`, so `current_round.bidder` is always non-None at `SelectTrump` time. The fallback is unreachable under current engine rules. If the engine ever changes to allow `SelectTrump` without a prior bid (e.g., a forced-bid scenario), the fallback would activate and set `bidder` to the `SelectTrump` player — which may or may not be correct.
findings.impact: No current bug. Dead code that could mask a future engine behavior change.
findings.recommendation: Either document why the fallback exists (defensive coding for engine contract uncertainty) or remove it and add an assertion `assert current_round.bidder is not None` to make the invariant explicit.

residual_risks.description: The `assert round_.trump is not None` and `assert round_.bid_amount is not None` in `__spike_round` will raise `AssertionError` (not an HTTP error) if a completed round has a bidder but no trump/bid_amount. This can happen if the action log is corrupted (e.g., a `Bid` with amount > 0 was recorded but the game ended before `SelectTrump`). The assert pattern is consistent with the rest of the codebase (serialize.game, serialize.lobby use asserts for invariants). However, `AssertionError` is not caught by the game exception handler and would surface as a 500 with no structured error body. Risk is low given the engine enforces the invariant, but a corrupted DB document could trigger it.

The `rounds` property performs a full action-walking replay (O(n) engine operations) on every call. The `spike_game` serializer calls `m_game.rounds` once and `m_game.status`, `m_game.winner`, `m_game.scores`, `m_game.active_player_id` separately — each of which may also replay or re-read engine state. For long games (21+ rounds, 334+ actions verified), this is a non-trivial computation. No caching exists. If the spike endpoint is called frequently or in a hot path, this could cause latency issues. Not a correctness bug but a performance risk.

The `Round` dataclass is mutable (not frozen), unlike the frozen dataclasses used for internal actions. The `rounds` property returns `Round` objects that are still mutable after being returned. If a caller modifies a returned `Round` (e.g., appends to `tricks` or changes `bidder`), it would not affect the game state (since `Round` is not connected to the engine), but it could cause subtle bugs in tests that reuse the same `Round` object. Currently no caller mutates returned rounds, but the lack of immutability is a latent risk.

testing_gaps.description: No test covers the case where `client_player_id` is not in the game (spectator scenario). The current behavior (all hands as counts, no queued actions, no crash) is untested. A test should verify either that spectators get a 403 (if that's the intent) or that they get a valid degraded response (if spectator access is acceptable).

No test covers the spike endpoint for a game in TRICKS phase with an in-progress trick (1-3 plays made). The active round trick visibility test only covers BIDDING phase. A test should verify that an in-progress trick appears in the active round's `tricks` list with the correct plays and `winning_play` (current leader).

No test covers the spike endpoint for a game in DISCARD or TRUMP_SELECTION phase. The `SpikeActiveRound.status` Literal includes these values but they are not exercised in the integration tests. A test should verify the correct status string is returned for each phase.

The `test_active_round_has_bid_history` test asserts `len(round_.bid_history) >= 0` — this is a tautology that always passes and provides no coverage. It should assert `len(round_.bid_history) > 0` (since NaiveCpu players auto-bid before p1's turn in the test setup, producing at least 3 bids).

No test verifies that the `rounds` property's score summing correctly handles duplicate player entries in the engine's score list. The `test_completed_game_scores_sum_to_cumulative` test validates the end result (sum == g.scores) but does not explicitly test the deduplication logic. A unit test with a mock engine round containing duplicate score entries would make this invariant explicit.
